title: Prompt Engineering vs Context Engineering
concept: Prompting shapes instructions; context engineering shapes the entire information environment the model reasons over.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, you learned to treat the context window like a...

options.text: budget of scarce tokens that must be allocated deliberately
unlimited storage bin with no limits

exercises.explain: The context window has limited space, so every token you put in it competes with every other token. Treating it like a budget means deciding deliberately what deserves a slot, which sets up today's lesson about controlling everything that fills that budget.

cards.title: Same Recipe, Different Kitchen
cards.body: Give a chef the world's best recipe card, but an empty fridge, and dinner still fails. The recipe is the prompt. The fridge, the ingredients, the tools on the counter, that's the context. Great instructions can't fix a missing ingredient.

cards.title: Two Different Jobs
cards.body: Prompt engineering crafts the instruction: the words that tell the model what to do right now. Context engineering designs the whole information environment around that instruction, like retrieved documents, memory, tool outputs, and history, so the model has what it needs to actually succeed.

cards.title: Director vs Producer
cards.body: Prompt engineering is like being the director, telling the actor exactly what to say in this one scene. Context engineering is like being the producer, controlling the script, the set, the props, and which actors even show up. The director's words only work well because the producer built the right environment.

cards.title: A Medical Chatbot
cards.body: Prompt engineering: writing 'Explain the diagnosis in simple, reassuring language.' Context engineering: making sure the patient's actual lab results, medical history, and doctor's notes are the documents sitting in the context window before that instruction is ever read.

cards.title: Myth: It's All Just 'Prompting'
cards.body: Myth: if the model's answer is bad, you just need cleverer wording. Truth: sometimes the wording is perfect but the model was never given the right documents, memory, or tool results. That's a context problem, not a phrasing problem, and no amount of clever wording can fix missing information.

cards.title: Instruction vs Environment
cards.body: Prompting is one message; context engineering is the whole system feeding the model.

left.text: Wording of the instruction: tone, format, examples, phrasing of the ask

right.text: What surrounds the instruction: retrieved docs, memory, tool outputs, history, budget

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a context engineering task rather than a prompt engineering task?

options.text: Rewording an instruction to be more polite
Deciding which retrieved documents get included in the context window
Adding an example of the desired output format to the instruction

exercises.explain: Deciding which documents to retrieve and include is about curating the information environment, not the wording of the instruction. The other options are all about phrasing or formatting the instruction itself, which is prompt engineering.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a model gives a wrong answer, rewriting the instruction more cleverly will always fix it.
exercises.explain: False. Sometimes the instruction is already perfectly clear, but the model never had access to the right information in its context window. In that case, no clever rewording helps, you need to fix the context, not the prompt.

exercises.sentence: Context engineering shapes the entire information environment, while prompt engineering mainly shapes the instruction's wording.
exercises.explain: Prompt engineering is focused on crafting the words of the ask itself, while context engineering handles everything else surrounding it, like documents, memory, and tools.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three explanations of prompt vs context engineering sounds AI-generated (generic, padded, lacking a concrete example)?

options.text: Prompt engineering focuses on optimizing the phrasing of instructions, while context engineering encompasses a holistic approach to curating comprehensive informational inputs, ultimately enhancing overall model performance and user satisfaction.
Prompt engineering writes the question well; context engineering makes sure the right facts are even in the room to answer it.
Think of prompting as choosing your words in an email, and context engineering as making sure you cc'd the right people with the right attachments.

exercises.explain: Option A is vague and buzzword-heavy ('holistic approach,' 'comprehensive informational inputs,' 'enhancing overall performance') without giving a concrete picture, a common sign of generic AI padding. The other two options use specific, relatable images (a room with facts, an email with attachments) that make the distinction click.

exercises.prompt: A support bot gives wrong answers even though its final instruction is clear and well-written: 'Answer the customer's question using ONLY the provided documents.' What is the MOST likely context engineering fix?

options.text: Rewrite the instruction in bold capital letters for emphasis
Check whether the actual correct documents were retrieved and placed into the context window
Add three more adjectives describing how helpful the bot should be
Ask the same question again in a different tone

exercises.explain: The instruction is already clear, so the failure isn't a prompting problem, it's an information problem. If the right documents were never retrieved or never made it into the context window, the model literally cannot answer correctly no matter how the instruction is phrased. Context engineering fixes what information the model has access to, which is often the real root cause of bad answers, especially after you've already learned to budget the context window carefully.
